tweets.text:
Goldman notes the drop in 10yr Treasury yields has mostly been through term premium (the extra risk for carrying duration vs overnight rates) but says that may have run its course:

The long-end yield move has largely shown up as a compression in Treasury term premium, with only modest contribution from the expectations component of yields (Exhibit 1).

 That dynamic that has seen the yield curve move steadily flatter through the course of the month despite the back and forth in outright yield levels. While hawkish Fed communication has reinforced the curve shift, we think that the argument for continued long-end outperformance looks more challenging... We think a meaningful extension of the yield move would likely need to incorporate some shift in the distribution of risks around the Fed path.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/SQmufwV7jr):

10yr term premium (the extra risk compensation for carrying duration (length) in a bond)) as of the week of May 11th (it’s reported with a little lag) jumped to the highest since 2011 (it likely though softened off that some last https://t.co/SKdC4o0PFJ
tweets.full_text:

 That dynamic that has seen the yield curve move steadily flatter through the course of the month despite the back and forth in outright yield levels. While hawkish Fed communication has reinforced the curve shift, we think that the argument for continued long-end outperformance looks more challenging... tweets.text:
The % of manufacturing GDP that contracted fell sharply to just 2% (from 19% in April and 16% in March), while the share in strong contraction (PMI at or below 45, “a good barometer of overall manufacturing weakness”) also remained at 2% (same as April, down from 4% in March and well below 43% in Dec). 

16 of 18 industries overall expanded (up from 13 in Apr, same as March) led by printing, textile mills and nonmetallic minerals, while just one contracted (Wood Products).

But prices remained above 80 for a second month for the first time since April 2022. For a third straight month zero industries reported declining prices (16 reported higher prices), and no commodities appeared on the down list.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
ISM US Manufacturing PMI - May 2026
ISM manufacturing PMI accelerates to 4-year high with improving production, new orders, and employment with just 2% of manufacturing GDP contracting, but prices remain 2nd highest since 2022.

https://t.co/CajtWGUnSV https://t.co/XOYYh8bMKX

16 of 18 industries overall expanded (up from 13 in Apr, same as March) led by printing, textile mills and nonmetallic minerals, while just one contracted (Wood Products).

tweets.text:
BBG: Hedge funds purchased US equities at the fastest pace in six months last week driven by long buys and short covering in a combination of index and exchange-traded fund products.

Leverage metrics also reflected renewed risk appetite. US long/short net leverage rose to 55.3%, climbing to the 89th percentile on a one-year basis. The US Fundamental long/short ratio increased by 1.4%, and is now in the 99th percentile.

Financial stocks were the clearest beneficiary of last week’s rotation, attracting the largest net buying in almost six months, the Goldman traders said. Long purchases outpaced short sales by roughly 6.5 to one, led by payment stocks and, to a lesser extent, banks. This was partially offset by selling in consumer finance and capital markets.

Even with the latest inflows to financials, allocations to the sector remain deeply depressed, the Goldman team noted. “Gross and net allocations in financials as a percentage of total US Prime book are still essentially at their respective 5-year lows in the 1st percentiles,” the desk wrote.

The push into financials contrasted sharply with continued pressure on industrials, which were net sold for seven of the last eight weeks. Short exposure in the sector has reached the 90th percentile on a one-year basis, with most of the selling since February driven by short sales rather than long liquidation, the Goldman desk noted.

The latest hedge fund positioning data marks a notable shift from the more defensive posture that the Goldman desk noted two-thirds of the way through May.

https://t.co/Vxqeq1HGHJ

Long purchases outpaced short sales by roughly 6.5 to one, led by payment stocks and, to a lesser extent, banks. This was partially offset by selling in consumer finance and capital markets.

Even with the latest inflows to financials, allocations to the sector remain deeply depressed, the Goldman team noted. “Gross and net allocations in financials as a percentage of total US Prime book are still essentially at their respective 5-year lows in the 1st percentiles,” the desk wrote.

The push into financials contrasted sharply with continued pressure on industrials, which were net sold for seven of the last eight weeks. Short exposure in the sector has reached the 90th percentile on a one-year basis, with most of the selling since February driven by short sales rather than long liquidation, the Goldman desk noted.

tweets.text:
MarketWatch: DB sticks with 8,000 target for the SPX this year and predicts more volatile conditions.

“DB’s S&P 500 target of 8,000, set back in our November edition, now appears much more realistic than many expected at the time,” say strategists led by Jim Reid. "Even so, markets, companies and countries are likely to remain highly sensitive to shifts in sentiment around AI’s impact, with large swings likely in both directions."
